summary: Gruber shares a custom Mac OS X Snow Leopard service he built called 'Open URLs in Safari Tabs.' The service extracts all URLs from selected text in any application and opens them as tabs in a new Safari window, running in the background. He explains how Snow Leopard's overhauled Services menu made creating such tools easy via Automator and scripting languages. The post includes download instructions, a demo video, and the MIT-licensed Perl/AppleScript source code. He notes the service only works with Safari and requires Snow Leopard or later.
key_points: Snow Leopard's Services menu overhaul made it dramatically easier to create custom system-wide services using Automator and scripting languages
The service extracts URLs from any selected text using Gruber's own liberal URL-matching regex pattern and opens them all as Safari tabs
The service deliberately opens tabs in the background to avoid disrupting the user's current workflow
The tool is Safari-only but the underlying Perl script is open-sourced under the MIT license
money_quotes: One of my favorite improvements to Snow Leopard was the overhaul of the system-wide Services menu.
The script finds all the URLs in the selected text, opens a new Safari window, and creates a tab in that new window for each of the URLs.
I wrote the script so that Safari doesn't come to the front when the service is invoked — the new window and tabs are created in the background.
Best of all, you can easily create your own services using Automator and scripting languages like AppleScript, Perl, Ruby, and Python.
key_insight: Snow Leopard's Services menu overhaul turned Mac OS X into a powerful automation platform where users could easily build custom system-wide utilities from simple scripts.
